coredata.dc:title: Innovative eco-friendly microwave-assisted synthesis of S,N-CQDs as a sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for the analysis of the food preservative sodium benzoate in soft drink samples: Compliance with greenness metrics

coredata.dc:description: The current study introduces an eco-harmonious, one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of highly fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) using available, economic, and green natural starting materials in only five minutes. Taro (Colocasia esculenta) was used as a green carbon source, while chicken muscles were used as a sulfur and nitrogen source, for the first time. Full characterization of the synthesized S,N-CQDs was performed using different spectroscopic and microscopic approaches, including Energy-Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR), spectrofluorimetry, and UV–Vis spectrophotometry. When S,N-CQDs were exposed to UV light, they demonstrated strong blue fluorescence. Additionally, the synthesized S,N-CQDs showed good fluorescence emission at 426 nm when excited at 345 nm. This study introduces a novel, simple, and environment-friendly spectrofluorimetric method for determination of sodium benzoate (SB); a food and soft drink additive, with no need for using hazardous organic solvents or any pre-derivatization steps. This strategy depends on SB quenching effect on the native fluorescence of newly synthesized S,N-CQDs. This strategy is the first one for the spectrofluorimetric determination of SB with the use of S,N-CQDs as nanosensors. As the SB doesn’t have native fluorescent properties; the superiority of the developed study increases. The developed approach demonstrated good linear relationship within the range of 1.0–40.0 μg mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9998 and a detection limit of 0.25 µg mL−1. The proposed method was applied for determination of SB in commercial soft drink samples with high % recoveries ranging from 98.20 to 102.52 %. Evaluation of the eco-friendless of the developed approach was performed using AGREE and ComplexGAPI metrics, confirming minimum environmental effect and excellent greenness of the method. The ICH Q2 (R1) guidelines were applied for method validation. This work underscores the importance of green synthesis methods and illustrates the practical application of S,N-CQDs in food analysis.

head.ce:title: Innovative eco-friendly microwave-assisted synthesis of S,N-CQDs as a sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for the analysis of the food preservative sodium benzoate in soft drink samples: Compliance with greenness metrics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study introduces an eco-harmonious, one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of highly fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) using available, economic, and green natural starting materials in only five minutes. Taro (Colocasia esculenta) was used as a green carbon source, while chicken muscles were used as a sulfur and nitrogen source, for the first time. Full characterization of the synthesized S,N-CQDs was performed using different spectroscopic and microscopic approaches, including Energy-Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR), spectrofluorimetry, and UV–Vis spectrophotometry. When S,N-CQDs were exposed to UV light, they demonstrated strong blue fluorescence. Additionally, the synthesized S,N-CQDs showed good fluorescence emission at 426 nm when excited at 345 nm. This study introduces a novel, simple, and environment-friendly spectrofluorimetric method for determination of sodium benzoate (SB); a food and soft drink additive, with no need for using hazardous organic solvents or any pre-derivatization steps. This strategy depends on SB quenching effect on the native fluorescence of newly synthesized S,N-CQDs. This strategy is the first one for the spectrofluorimetric determination of SB with the use of S,N-CQDs as nanosensors. As the SB doesn’t have native fluorescent properties; the superiority of the developed study increases. The developed approach demonstrated good linear relationship within the range of 1.0–40.0 μg mL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9998 and a detection limit of 0.25 µg mL−1. The proposed method was applied for determination of SB in commercial soft drink samples with high % recoveries ranging from 98.20 to 102.52 %. Evaluation of the eco-friendless of the developed approach was performed using AGREE and ComplexGAPI metrics, confirming minimum environmental effect and excellent greenness of the method. The ICH Q2 (R1) guidelines were applied for method validation. This work underscores the importance of green synthesis methods and illustrates the practical application of S,N-CQDs in food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based Quantum dots (CQDs) had got a great interest in the last few decades due to their distinctive and unique optical characteristics that enabled their usage in various fields, as drug delivery, bio-imaging, and bio-sensing. Quantum dots were found out during electrophoresis process of single walled carbon nanotubes in the separation and purification step by Xu et al. [1]. Sulfur and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs) consist of carbonaceous non-toxic core doped by hetero atoms on their surface giving extraordinary luminescent emission properties and novel physical, chemical, and biological properties, such as high water dispersion, easy surface tunability, high photo-stability, and chemical inertness, making them safe and excellent biocompatible nanosensors [2,3]. These novel optical properties are due to quantum detention of electrons and gap carriers at dimensionality smaller than Bohr radius (less than 10 nm) [4]. The lower sized the QDs, the larger the band holes; due to the raise in the energy level difference between the high valence band and the low conveyance band. Therefore, more QDs excitation energy is needed, and when they get back to the ground state, more energy is released, accordingly [5–7]. Owing to their unique and tailored optical characteristics, S,N-CQDs became excellent alternative to organic fluorescent dyes. These characteristics include lack of photo-bleaching, lower photo-degradation rate, and slower decay rates of the excited state in QD than organic fluorescent dyes [8].

The two known synthesis strategies for CQDs are top-down and bottom-up synthesis. In top-down approach, bulky carbonaceous substances as graphite are split and processed through oxidative cleavage in multiple steps using strong chemicals like concentrated acids, hydrothermal methods, under high temperature and pressure conditions, ultrasound, microwave-assisted or electrochemical method to result in graphite oxide, that is crushed into QDs in nano-size. In bottom-up approach, dehydration, polymerization, and then carbonization, at high temperature, of any carbon based small molecules, natural resources, waste materials or green synthesis into CQDs, are performed through conventional hydrothermal methods, solvothermal, microwave irradiation, and dry heating [3]. In this work, the bottom-up approach was applied as it is simpler and easier than the top-down approach and without incorporating impurities during synthesis. The wet chemical synthesis route was followed, using conventional precipitation methods in the aqueous phase in a one-step synthesis technique with watchful control of the reaction parameters. Moreover, carbonization of the inexpensive natural precursors or bio-waste fulfills the expectations of the global claims for including green chemistry into laboratories’ culture [9]. The doping amount or the number of CQDs-surface functional groups and the type of doped hetero atoms like nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), sulfur (S), or phosphorus (P) greatly enhance CQDs' fluorescence quality as they affect surface structure and electron density of the CQDs, increasing the fluorescence intensity [10]. Doping of the CQDs with S and N, was more important and common than doping with other hetero atoms, due to the nearly similar atomic size to that of C (N: 0.75, C: 0.77) and nearly the same electronegativity as that of C atom (C: 2.55, S: 2.58) [11], that improved CQDs performance and their optical properties and broadened their application. Investigating the literature, CQDs as highly fluorescent probes were efficiently used for the determination of many pharmaceuticals and chemicals in various matrices [12–24].

In this work, an economic, simple, and ecofriendly method was proposed for preparation of S,N-CQDs applying a rapid microwave-assisted synthesis procedure using precursors of natural origin. Chicken muscles were used as sulfur and nitrogen source because of being rich in cysteine and methionine amino acids [25,26], and taro; a tropical plant with edible root-like corms called (Colocasia esculenta) from Arum family (Araceae) was used as a green carbon source, resulting in sensitive photoluminescent probe for the quantitative estimation of sodium benzoate (SB) preservative in carbonated beverages.

The preservative E211, sodium benzoate, was approved by FDA [27] to be used as a safe preservative in beverages, processed food products, and pharmaceuticals like cough syrup, in concentrations up to 0.1 % by weight to prevent deterioration and increase shelf life. The allowed daily intake (ADI) of SB by FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA) was 0–5 mg/kg body weight that was re-established as 0–20 mg/kg in the committee’s 92nd meeting [28]. It is best used as preservative for acidic food products, such as lemon juice, pickles, soda, and salad dressing [29]. Although SB was declared to be a safe preservative, it might be converted into a potential carcinogen, especially upon mixing with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) that is added to or present naturally in beverages like bottled lemon juice, soda, and other soft drinks, that converts SB into benzene; a well known carcinogen [30]. Exposure to elevated temperature and light stimulate benzene production [30]. In the United Kingdom, SB was replaced in the majority of soft drinks, by sodium sorbate [31]. Moreover, some studies reported SB harmfulness when used for treatment of some disorders such as autism, neurodegenerative disorders, schizophrenia, and pain, as it was found to reduce fertility, have mutagenic effects, and disrupt hormones [32].

Accordingly, the aim of this study was the synthesis of extremely green, highly sensitive, easily prepared, and cost-effective S,N-CQDs of natural origin usable as a fluorescent nanoprobe for detecting and targeting analytes of a wide range of chemicals exemplified by SB determination in soft drink by its quantitative quenching effect on the S,N-CQDs fluorescence. The prepared S,N-CQDs sensor was usable for up to 12 months with no change or fluorescence fading upon keeping in the refrigerator. Upon comparing the suggested spectrofluorimetric technique with other reported methods such as spectrophotometric [33,34], Immunoassay fluorimetry [35], HPLC-UV [36–45], HPLC-MS-MS [45], and electrophoresis methods [46] for determination of SB, the proposed method had several merits over them, like simplicity, cost effectiveness, and eco-friendliness with no need for sophisticated apparatus, large volumes of organic solvents, or harsh experimental conditions. Moreover, the suggested method was more cost effective and more simple, utilizing available natural materials compared to the only reported fluorimetric method; the fluorescence polarization immunoassay (FPIA) method [37], that based on apolyclonal antibody that utilized complicated conjugation steps and a highly expensive and sophisticated apparatus for determination of SB. Although there were several methods reported for S,N-CQDs synthesis from green sources, it was the first time for taro and chicken flesh to be used for synthesis of S,N-CQDs, adding merits for the proposed work, due to their natural origin, availability, being economic and green, that increases practicability of the method over other green reported ones for synthesis of S,N-CQDs. Moreover, the proposed method was the first one that utilized S,N-CQDs as sensitive fluorescent nanosensors for determination of SB in soft drink sample through its quenching effect on the native fluorescence of S,N-CQDs. The developed spectrofluorimetric method is the first one for the analysis of SB with no need for pre-derivatization, hazardous chemicals, or complicated procedures. A summary of the comparison between the developed method and the previously reported ones was sum up in Table 1 .

Sodium benzoate was kindly obtained as a gift from Pharco Pharmaceuticals for Pharmaceutical Industries (Amriya, Alexandria, Egypt). Analytical grade Quinine sulfate (QS) was used (Sigma, Gmbh, 160 Germany). Chicken muscles and Taro, were purchased from local market, (5 g and 10 g, respectively, were finely and separately chopped, then homogenized together). Carbonated soft drink sample (320-mL) was purchased from a local supermarket. Double-distilled water was used for preparation of S,N-CQDs, and as a diluting solvent. Universal Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) with variable pH values (2.1–12) was prepared, as described in literature [47] by adjusting the pH of a mixture of 0.04 M phosphoric acid, 0.04 M boric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide.

ce:list-item.ce:para: A UV lamp (λ = 254 nm /366 nm) (Vilber Lourmat; VL-6.LC, Cedex 1, France) was used.
A domestic SMART MW20WCRK microwave, 700 Watt, was used for synthesis of the S,N-CQD.

A double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer 1601 PC (Shimadzu®, Kyoto, Japan) and UVPC software v. 3.7. were used for UV-absorbance measurements with 2 quartz cuvettes of 1 cm path length and 2800 nm/min wavelength-scanning speed.

Cary Eclipse® Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, USA) was used for fluorescence quantifications at 600 nm/min scanning speed, and it was operated at 700 V-mode with 5 nm slit width for both excitation and emission.
Ultrasonic bath, Sonix, TVSS-series (CA, USA) was used.
pH meter (Consort, NVP- 901, Belgium) was used for adjusting different pH values.
Thermo-Fisher Scientific Nicolet® iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Massachusetts, US) was used for Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra recording from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with KBr pellets.
High speed Centrifuge (model 2-16P, Germany) up to 6000 rpm was also used.

PANanalytical® X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) instrument model X׳Pert PRO along with secondary Monochromator, Cu-radiation (λ = 1.542 Å) at 35 M.A., 45 K.V., and a scanning speed 0.04°/sec was used, and the diffraction peaks within 2θ = 2° and 60°, corresponding spacing (d, Å) and with relative intensities (I/Io) were measured.

A JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) playing at 200 kV was used to characterize the morphology of the synthesized S,N-CQDs using carbon-coated Cu-grid (200 mesh).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Jeol JSM, 6510LV, Japan, and an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Oxford-Xmax 20, were operated to verify elemental composition of the synthesized S,N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: A standard stock solution of SB was prepared in double-distilled water in concentration of 100.0 µg mL−1, then, further diluted with double-distilled water for preparing different working solutions covering a concentration ranging of 1–40.0 µg mL−1.

Synthesis of S,N-doped CQDs was carried out using one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis methodology. One g minced taro and 0.5 g minced chicken muscles were well homogenized and mixed together along with 20 mL double-distilled water. The mixture was heated in a domestic microwave (700 W) for 5 min until dark brown residue was obtained, then it was allowed to cool at room temperature. The product was then dissolved in 50 mL double-distilled water with continuous stirring. The solution was twice filtered using Whatman® filter paper then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to remove any un-dissolved suspended particles. For more purification the S,N-CQDs solution was syringe-filtered using 45 µm filter. A dark orange colored solution was produced of highly fluorescent S,N-CQDs formed, which was kept at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrofluorimetric measurements and calibration curve construction
ce:section.ce:para: Into a series of 10-mL volumetric flasks, increasing volumes of SB standard stock solution (in a range of concentrations of 1.0–40.0 µg mL−1) were separately transferred, then each mixed with 200.0 µL of S,N-CQDs and 1 mL BRB buffer (pH 10). The volume was then adjusted to the mark with double-distilled water, then the fluorescence intensity was measured after an incubation time of 5 min at 40 °C using temperature controlled water bath. The fluorescence intensities readings (F) were recorded at 426 nm upon excitation at 345 nm. The S,N-CQDs fluorescence quenching (F0-F) by SB serial concentrations, was recorded at 40 °C, against a blank experiment measurement (F0). A Calibration curve was created by plotting (F0- F) versus SB concentrations (µg mL−1) and the regression equation then was derived.

The quantum yield of S,N-CQDs was calculated as per the following equation Eq. (1) [48,49]:
(1)
Φ S,N-CQDs = Φ QS ( F
S,N-CQDs/ F
QS) x ( Ƞ
S,N-CQDs/ Ƞ
QS) 2 × ( A
QS/ A
S,N-CQDs)
where, Φ; quantum yield, F; the integrated measured emission intensity, η; the refractive index of the used solvent (this value equals 1 for double-distilled water), A; the absorbance, and QS; Quinine Sulfate; the reference standard used. It was prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 and possessed QY of 0.54 at 350 nm. Quinine sulfate and S,N-CQDs standard diluted solutions having absorbance less than 0.05 were used, to neglect and avoid inner filter effect (IFE) at excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SB in soft drink samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium benzoate is frequently added as a preservative to soft drinks. 100 mL aliquot of the commercial soft drink samples (320 mL), were degassed in a sonicator for 1 hr, then 10.0 mL of the degassed sample were syringe filtered through 0.22 µm pore sized filter. Three different aliquots (0.5, 1, and 1.5 mL) were separately transferred into 10 mL-volumetric flasks and the volume was completed to the mark with double distilled water. Then, 1 mL aliquot of each solution was separately transferred to 10 mL-volumetric flask, mixed with 200.0 µL of S,N-CQDs and 1 mL BRB buffer (pH 10). The volume was adjusted to the mark with double-distilled water, and the intensities of the fluorescence of the solutions were measured after an incubation time of 5 min at 40 °C. The procedure mentioned under Section ''2.5.'' was then utilized for determination of SB nominal concentration. Standard addition technique was performed by spiking of 1 mL of degassed soft drink sample with variable aliquots (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 mL) of pure standard SB stock solution (100.0 µg mL−1), then mixed with 200.0 µL of S,N-CQDs and 1 mL BRB buffer (pH 10). The volume was adjusted to the mark with double-distilled water, and the fluorescence measurements of those mixtures were performed after incubation time of 5 min at 40 °C. The same fluorescence measurement procedure mentioned under section ''2.5.'' was then utilized for determination of SB concentration in those samples.

Two greenness assessment approaches were utilized for evaluation of method’s eco-friendliness, namely, Analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) [50], and the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [51].

It was newly developed online tool for greenness evaluation software that was firstly presented by Pena-Pereira [50], where an auto-generated figure segmented into 12 parts, that explored the reagents amount and toxicities, procedure’s practical steps, waste production, and overall energy consumption by the method. The summation of scoring in AGREE is a part of unity which ranges from 0 to 1 represented by colors from green to red, and the total score was presented in pictogram's center.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI)
ce:section.ce:para: ComplexGAPI is an evaluation index pictogram [51], that provides a comprehensive overview of the green character of an analytical method that included the use of substances produced prior to the analysis procedure step, such as S,N-CQDs in this work. The pre-analysis practice, that included solvents, conditions, and equipment evaluation, was represented as a hexagon at the bottom of an upper pictogram that consisted of pentagonal fields for the evaluation of the greenness of the suggested method itself. Those fields included instrumentations, energy consumption, waste generation, procedures, health hazard of reagents and solvents, and the purpose of the method, whether for qualification or quantitation. The environmental impact was represented by three colors, green, red, and yellow for low, high, and medium impact, respectively.

The adopted approach for microwave-aided synthesis of S,N-CQDs allowed for complete synthesis achievement in only few minutes using a domestic microwave of low energy (700 W). Another key merit in the synthesis steps was the use of natural, green, cost-effective, and renewable feedstock of chicken muscles, as S- and N-source, and Taro, as a green C-source. The easily accessibility, wide availability, and greenness of the starting materials guarantee the wide applicability of the proposed synthesis procedure.

Under UV lamp light (365 nm), the orange colored solution of S,N-CQDs showed strong blue fluorescence (Fig. S1 inset), that lasted stable against photo-bleaching for several hours. The S,N-CQDs exhibited remarkable chemical stability and reusability upon storage at 4 °C without any noticeable precipitation and with negligible change in the quantum yield as demonstrated by its consistent fluorescence intensity over an extended period of more than 12 months. The S,N-CQDs UV-absorption spectrum in double-distilled water exhibited a maximum wavelength at 280 nm, as shown in Fig. S1. Also, the S,N-CQDs optimum fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were found to be 345 and 426 nm, respectively, as presented in Fig. 1 a. The dependency of the S,N-CQDs emission on the excitation wavelength was studied by changing the excitation wavelength in variable range from 330-400 nm, and investigating its effect on the emission. The emission spectra exhibited dependency on excitation as it exhibited bathochromic shift as shown in Fig. 1b, and the highest emission intensity was observed at 345 nm. Compared with the reference QS, the quantum yield of S,N-CQDs was also calculated, as referred under Section ''2.6.'', and it was found to be as high as 46 %.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to check S,N-CQDs chemical structure and surface functional groups. Several distinct observed bands that confirmed successful incorporation of copious O, S, and N-containing functional groups on the surface of S,N-CQDs, maintaining improved polarity and water solubility. The broad strong band at 3428 cm−1 was attributed to stretching modes of O–H and/or N–H. Vibrational bands at 2924 cm−1 and 2856 cm−1 were due to aliphatic C-H stretching, while the band at 1647 cm−1 was due to amide CO bending or CN stretching vibration. Band at 1538 cm−1 was assigned to N-O stretching, while SO sulfone and sulfoxide were responsible for 1384 cm−1 and 1027 cm−1 bands, respectively. Band at 1240 cm−1 was also observed that assigned to CS stretching (Fig. 2 a) [52,53]. High resolution TEM analysis was used to further investigate S,N-CQDs morphological features, surface characters, and size. From results in Fig. 2b, it was found that, S,N-CQDs were regularly distributed with spherical shape and their sizes ranged from 0.1 to 3 nm. The prepared particles were amorphous as revealed by the electron diffraction of selected area that showed diffuse rings. Moreover, S,N-CQDs crystallization was investigated using X-ray Diffraction, (scanning speed of 0.04°/s was used), showing diffraction peak at position 20° (°2 Theta angle of detector) (Cu), relative intensities (I/Io), and diffraction chart were obtained, showing that the synthesized S,N-CQDs are amorphous, as presented in Fig. 2c. Energy-Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) with Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) were used for investigating elemental composition and ratio of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur atoms of the S,N-CQDs (Fig. 2d). The obtained results revealed that the synthesized S,N-CQDs contain about 30.67 % carbon, 9.20 % nitrogen, 57.22 % oxygen, and 2.90 % sulfur, confirming the sufficient doping of CQDs with sulfur and nitrogen. Moreover, the distribution of those elements over the S,N-CQDs nanoparticles was investigated by elemental mapping that localized presence of C, N, O and S with their overlay image (Fig. 2e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of S,N-CQDs fluorescence quenching by SB
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of S,N-CQDs-fluorescence quenching was classified into three main categories; static quenching, dynamic quenching, and inner filter effect (IFE) [54]. The quenching is considered to be static, if the quencher forms a complex with the fluorescent ground stated sensor molecules without excitation, and inhibits it from light absorption to stimulate electrons to the excited state. In other words, it prevents a proportion of the fluorophore from reaching emissive excited state by forming complex with it, consequently, the lifetime of fluorescence doesn't change at all [55]. While dynamic quenching is due to collisions or interaction of the quencher with the fluorescent probe in the excited state that leads to deactivation by returning to the ground state, and decrease in its apparent excited state’s life time [54–57]. Both static and dynamic quenching are greatly affected by temperature. In case of static mechanism, the stability of the fluorophore-quencher complex greatly decreases by raising temperature, decreasing its quenching effect as the free proportion of the fluorophore increases. On the other hand, dynamic quenching process is temperature sensitive as it relies on diffusion and solvent viscosity, so it increases by elevating temperature [57]. The quenching mechanism in this work was investigated and characterized by Stern-Volmer analysis. It describes the static and dynamic intermolecular deactivation related to the quencher concentration. Stern-Volmer analysis was performed, by subsequent increasing temperature and observing its effect on fluorescence quenching of S,N-CQDs in presence of SB, as per Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 2).
(2)
F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q]
where (F0/F) is the ratio of FI of S,N-CQDs in the absence and existence of SB at variable temperature settings (293, 303, and 313 K), Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is SB molar concentration. As shown in Fig. 3 a, increasing the concentrations of SB (1.0–40.0μg mL−1) significantly decreased S,N-CQDs fluorescence. Results showed that, upon increasing temperature, 75 % of the emission fluorescence of S,N-CQDs at 426 nm was quenched in existence of 69.4 μM (10.0μg mL−1) of SB, confirming the dynamic quenching interaction mechanism, hence the excited state S,N-CQDs went down to the original ground state by collision with SB, and Ksv values increased with increasing temperature. A Plot of F0 /F against SB molar concentration at different temperature is shown in Fig. 3b. Moreover, the inner filter effect was also studied [58], as shown in Fig. S2a, to some extent, there was an overlap between the UV- absorption spectrum of SB and the excitation spectrum of S,N-CQDs, but not the emission one, suggesting occurrence of primary IFE. The correction of fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs for a probable IFE was calculated using the following equation (3).
(3) Fcorr = Fobs. × 10(Aex+Aem)/2
where, Fobs. and Fcorr. represent corrected and observed S,N-CQDs fluorescence intensities before and after excluding inner filter effect, respectively. Aex and Aem are the values of absorbance at wavelengths of S,N-CQDs excitation and emission, respectively. The fluorescence suppression efficiency (%E) for the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities are calculated using the following equation (4):
(4)
%E = [1 − (F∕F0 )] × 100

The %E for both observed and corrected fluorescence intensities are plotted against SB concentration and the results in Fig. S2b, showed that primary IFE of SB also affects the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs causing 54 % suppression efficiency. Consequently, IFE is a complementary quenching mechanism in this work, and the main quenching effect is the dynamic quenching mechanism.

The starting materials’ ratio and the duration of S,N-CQDs synthesis in the microwave were carefully optimized. Taro and chicken flesh in ratios of 1:1, 2:1, and 1:2 were tried, and the FIs of the resulted S,N-CQDs from each ratio, were recorded. It was found that the ratio of 2:1 provided the maximum FI and QY of the synthesized S,N-CQDs between them (Fig. S3a). This is because the carbon source (taro) for quantum dots core, is required in slight more amount than the sulfur and nitrogen sources (chicken flesh) that is needed just for doping. The synthesis duration was also optimized, where 5 min was the optimum time, resulting in the highest FI and QY of the synthesized S,N-CQDs. Shorter time; 2, 3, and 4 min, resulted in incompletely charred residues and low S,N-CQDs yield, while longer time didn't significantly enhance the QY, and is not recommended in terms of greenness. Therefore, 5 min was used as the optimum synthesis time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for analysis of SB
ce:section.ce:para: In order to increase the sensitivity and achieve the maximum one, in addition to promote the optimum experimental conditions, the effect of different analytical parameters on the quenching effect of SB (10.0 µg mL−1) on the synthesized S,N-CQDs was studied. These conditions include incubation time, pH of the media, and temperature.

The influence of incubation period was investigated by recording fluorescence quenching effect (F0-F) of SB after mixing with S,N-CQDs after 0‘, 5‘, 10‘, 15‘, 20‘, 30‘, and 60‘. Results showed in Fig. S3b proved that the maximum quenching efficiency was reached after 5 min, and remained stable without a significant change for at least 1 hr., which added another merit for the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of the media
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of pH was investigated by performing the experiments over pH range of 2.1–12 with BRB, and recording its effect on the efficiency of fluorescence quenching (F0-F). As demonstrated by Fig. S3c, SB maximum florescence quenching effect was attained at alkaline pH, and pH 10 was the optimum one, resulting in the maximum quenching.

The effect of temperature on efficiency of fluorescence quenching (F0-F) by SB on S,N-CQDs was studied over temperature range from 20-50 °C. It was found that quenching efficiency was increased by increasing temperature, and 40 °C was chosen as the optimum one (Fig. S3d).
ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines [59] were followed for validation of the proposed method.

The quenching effect of SB on the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs probe was fitted by graphing SB concentrations (µg mL−1) versus the difference (ΔF) between S,N-CQDs fluorescence in absence (F0) and presence (F) of SB (ΔF = F0 − F), and the regression equation was then computed. Linearity of SB was correlated in the concentration range of 1.0–40.0 µg mL−1 obeying the following regression Eq. (5):
(5)
F0 − F = 12.732x + 43.281 (r2 = 0.9998)
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the probe without and in presence of known concentration of SB, respectively, X is the concentration in µg mL−1, and r2 is the linear regression coefficient. The value of r2 near unity indicated excellent linearity of the developed method (Table 2 ).

The detectable and quantitation limits of SB were estimated using the following equations (6 and 7) [59]:
(6)
LOD = 3.3 σ/S
(7)
LOQ = 10 σ/S
where σ is the standard deviation for the regression line y-intercept, while S is the slope. LOD and LOQ values listed in Table 2 confirmed the acceptable sensitivity concerned with the proposed study and its reliability for assay of SB in the commercial soft drink beverage.

Evaluating the method’s precision, including both inter-day and intra-day precisions was a must through analyzing three different concentrations of SB (10.0, 20.0, 30.0 µg mL−1) (in three individual replicates) within the same day and on three successive days, respectively. Acceptable results were obtained as revealed from the low % RSD and % error values (Table S1). Accuracy was assessed via comparing the proposed study’s results for SB assay in raw substance with those of the reported one [33]. Results in Table S2 stated that there was no significant variation in both accuracy and precision of the proposed and reported methods, which was revealed from statistically analyzing data with Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test, respectively [60]. Moreover, the recovery study was done to indicate the accuracy of the proposed method. It was performed by fortification of diluted samples with pure standard SB at three different concentration levels (5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 µg mL−1), as shown in Table 3 . Satisfactory SB recoveries were obtained, ranging from 98.20-102.52 %, confirming the accuracy of the suggested method within the acceptable range with negligible spiked sample matrix effect on SB analysis.

Robustness of the suggested method was evaluated by monitoring the influence of minor deliberate changes, one at a time, on experimental parameters that affected quenching efficiency of S,N-CQDs fluorescence by SB, and investigating its effect on % RSD values. The impact of incubation time of the synthesized probe with SB (5 ± 1 min), pH of the media (10 ± 0.1), and temperature (40 ± 1°C) was studied and showed no significant impact on %RSD values, indicating the method's robustness (Table 2).

Sodium benzoate concentration in different soft drink samples, other than the one previously tested (7Up® sample), was determined by the proposed method, and there was no significant difference between the obtained results, proving the selectivity of the proposed method toward SB determination in the matrix of different soft drink samples without interference. For further proving the method’s selectivity, different suspected interfering substances were tested. Selectivity study was performed on other preservatives, or additives, rather than SB that were probable to be present in the soft drink matrix, such as, glucose, citric acid, sodium citrate, and potassium sorbate, in addition to different mono, di, and trivalent metal ions like, Na+, K+, Cu+2, Zn+2, Al+3, and Fe+3. Results presented in Fig. S4, showed that there was no interference from common additives or metal ions that might be present in soft drink sample. The selectivity of the proposed method was further proven via its ability of determining SB in soft drink samples with negligible matrix effect and high % recoveries, as stated from results in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SB in commercial carbonated soft drink samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method, it was utilized for the quantification of SB in different soft drink samples from the local market. Soft drink samples were appropriately diluted to prepare 9 replicates at 3 different dilution levels. The derived regression equations for SB were used for calculating its concentration in the tested samples. As listed in Table4, the tested soft drink samples were found to contain 1.98–2.12 g/L SB with % recovery ranging from 123.80 to 132.90 % compared to reference limit [28]. From these results, it was found that, the synthesized S,N-CQDs can be efficiently applied for SB determination, consequently, unveiling their promising potential in SB tracing in such matrix of soft drink, without interference from other constituents.

At the first glance, performing short time, low energy microwave-assisted synthetic procedure, and using renewable and natural C, S, and N sources for S,N-CQDs, gave the proposed analytical method the merits of being economic, safe, and eco-friendly. Greenness assessment was performed using AGREE and complexGAPI metrics, as mentioned under Section ''2.8.''. The AGREE colored pictogram of the developed method was created with a total score of 0.77, indicating acceptable greenness of the developed assay, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. In addition, the proposed method's complexGAPI pictogram (Fig. 4b) showed green color for most of its segments, confirming the excellent greenness and eco-friendliness of the suggested method and the synthesis procedure of S,N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with the previously reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: Although several methods were reported for quantification of SB in different matrices [33–46], the proposed method had several merits over them (Table 1). It was the first method that utilized S,N-CQDs for determination of SB in soft drink sample. It provided ecofriendly microwave-aided synthesis of highly-fluorescent S,N-CQDs with high quantum yield, within only 5 min, so, it was time, cost and energy-saving (only of 700 W) and utilized natural, renewable, cheap, available, and self-passivation agents (taro and chicken flesh for the first time), not hazardous chemical compounds compared to other methods. Moreover, it is favored to the reported fluorescence polarization immunoassay (FPIA) method [35], in using cheap and available, natural materials and simple procedures unlike the reported FPIA that was highly complicated and expensive based on apolyclonal antibody that utilized complicated conjugation steps for quantification of SB. Moreover, it did not apply harsh experimental conditions comparing to the hydrothermal and solvothermal routes of CQDs synthesis. Another aspect of excellence of the suggested method was the simplicity and greenness of the procedure used for determination of SB in soft drink sample, consuming no organic solvents, unlike the reported chromatographic methods that consumed large volume of hazardous organic solvents and sophisticated apparatus. Therefore, the developed method outperforms the previously reported ones for analysis of SB.

In the proposed study, S,N-CQDs were successfully synthesized from food-inspired sources with no use of organic solvents within few minutes. Various approaches were used for S,N-CQDs characterization to explore their optical and structural properties. The characterized S,N-CQDs served as a novel turn off fluorescent probe for SB determination, for the first time, through the quantitative and selective dynamic quenching mechanism of SB on the native fluorescence of S,N-CQDs, in addition to inner filter effect. In spite of reporting several green synthesis methods of S,N-CQDs, the proposed method had the merits of using available, cheap and natural S, N and C sources for synthesis of S,N-CQDs. Moreover, the developed method is eco-friendly, rapid, time-saving, using the affordable microwave–assisted synthesis in only 5 min, in addition to cost effectiveness of the procedure instruments. It was fully validated following ICH guidelines. The novel probe was utilized, for the first time, for SB determination in pure form and soft drink samples. Moreover, satisfactory AGREE and complexGAPI greenness assessment tools’ results were obtained. This work underscores the importance of green synthesis methods and illustrates the practical application of S,N-CQDs in food analysis. There were many future perspectives for this research, which will be the subject of a future work. The method could be improved by expanding of the starting materials and doping substances, by using other animal flesh as doping elements to enhance the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized carbon quantum dots. By addressing anticipated future trends and advancements, S,N-CQDs-based technologies possess the capacity to significantly improve sensor technology, food safety, and public health. The integration of green chemistry principles with advanced nanotechnology presents significant promise for addressing global pollution and resource management challenges.
